text: To be honest I don't really know, I've just always played it. I grew up in Dublin, Ireland.
Dublin, I suppose traditionally over the last 60 years, even though it's getting back into
it now, wouldn't have been always a hurling stronghold. Other sports would have been bigger
like soccer, rugby or Gaelic football, but there's been a huge revolution over the last
6-7 years. Brian is on my floor in Knowles and he was
like hey you should come out hurling, it's not just a guys sport. Initially I was skeptical
because it seemed really intense, but I came out and it was a lot of fun.
I've met Brian because of the international exchange program and he talked about hurling,
that it's the Irish national sport and he got really excited when he was talking about
that and he showed me a video on YouTube and I got really excited too when I saw the video.
So I just decided to join his club.
It combines all the aspects of all the sports that I've done, I've done a lot of sports,
all the sports that I love. It's the oldest sport that I know of, it seems like the world
knows of. And it was easy to pick up for me just because as Americans we play a lot of
the sports that combine into hurling, so it makes it fun.
Well see where I come from in Ireland, it's a place called Kilkenny, so more or less hurling
is like a second religion. Some people might say it's more important, but not that it's
a lack of faith or anything, it's just you grow up and there's two things really in life,
it's the culture and the culture of the sport.
My Irish teacher is the coach, I have Nisha from my Irish 1 class and he was actually
looking for signatures just to be recognized by ASUM. And so I just kind of signed up because
it was a name on a list and then he started talking about it and he showed us clips in
class and I was like wow that looks like fun. So I came and checked it out and I liked it
so I stuck around.
